text: What does it mean for a molecule to be hydrophilic?
choices: It repels water because it is nonpolar
It tends to interact well with water, often because it is polar or charged
It contains only carbon and hydrogen
It can exist only inside membranes
explain: Hydrophilic molecules interact favorably with water because they are polar or charged.

text: What does it mean for a molecule to be hydrophobic?
choices: It interacts strongly with water because it is charged
It tends to avoid water because it is largely nonpolar
It is always acidic
It is made only of amino acids
explain: Hydrophobic molecules are largely nonpolar and do not mix well with water.

text: What is the main role of peptide bonds in biology?
choices: They link amino acids together in proteins
They connect nucleotides in DNA
They join monosaccharides in starch
They hold phospholipid heads to water molecules
explain: Peptide bonds covalently join amino acids to form the primary structure of proteins.

text: Why is the sequence of nucleotides in DNA important?
choices: It determines the order of amino acids in proteins
It determines whether water is polar
It changes the number of elements in the organism
It is important only in RNA, not DNA
explain: DNA sequence encodes information specifying the order of amino acids in proteins.

text: A scientist compares two solutions containing equal amounts of similar-sized molecules. Molecule A dissolves readily in water, while Molecule B does not. Molecule A has several hydroxyl groups, while Molecule B is dominated by hydrocarbon chains. Which conclusion is most reasonable?
choices: Molecule A is more polar and can form more favorable interactions with water
Molecule B is more hydrophilic because hydrocarbon chains attract water strongly
Molecule A must be a protein because it dissolves in water
Molecule B must contain no carbon
explain: Hydroxyl groups increase polarity and hydrogen-bonding with water; hydrocarbon chains are hydrophobic.

text: A researcher hydrolyzes a protein into its monomers and then rebuilds a new protein with the same monomers in a different order. Why can the rebuilt protein have a different function?
choices: Changing the amino acid order can change folding and therefore function
Proteins with the same monomers must always have the same function
Hydrolysis converts amino acids into nucleotides
The rebuilt protein will become a lipid if the order changes
explain: Primary sequence determines folding; reordering amino acids can alter structure and function.

text: A DNA-binding protein has a positively charged region that interacts with negatively charged DNA. A mutation replaces several positively charged amino acids in that region with neutral amino acids. Which outcome is most likely?
choices: The protein may bind DNA less strongly because electrostatic attraction is reduced
The protein will gain more phosphodiester bonds
The DNA will become nonpolar
The protein must become single-stranded
explain: Reducing positive charge weakens electrostatic interactions with DNA's negatively charged backbone, lowering binding affinity.

text: Two carbohydrate polymers are made entirely of glucose. Polymer X is highly branched and used for energy storage. Polymer Y is linear and used structurally. Which explanation best fits these differences?
choices: The arrangement of monomers and the types of glycosidic linkages influence structure and function
Linear glucose polymers cannot have any covalent bonds
Branched polymers cannot store energy
Structural carbohydrates must be made of amino acids
explain: Linkage orientation and branching produce different polymer architectures and biological roles despite identical monomers.

text: A protein remains a continuous chain after heating, but it no longer catalyzes its reaction efficiently. Which explanation is most reasonable?
choices: Heat likely disrupted the enzyme’s higher-order structure, altering the active site
Heat must have changed every amino acid into a sugar
The protein lost its primary structure entirely
The protein became more stable because it stopped reacting
explain: Denaturation affects secondary/tertiary structure and active site geometry without breaking peptide bonds.

text: A cell membrane contains many phospholipids with unsaturated tails. Compared with a membrane rich in saturated tails, what is the best prediction?
choices: The membrane will tend to be more fluid because the unsaturated tails pack less tightly
The membrane will lose all hydrophilic regions
The membrane will become a carbohydrate layer
The membrane can no longer act as a barrier in water
explain: Kinks from double bonds reduce tight packing and increase bilayer fluidity.

text: A biologist finds that an unknown molecule contains carbon, hydrogen, oxygen, nitrogen, and sulfur. It is built from monomers that each contain amino and carboxyl groups. Which conclusion is most supported?
choices: The molecule is likely a protein, and sulfur may come from certain amino acid side chains
The molecule is likely DNA because DNA always contains sulfur
The molecule is likely a triglyceride because triglycerides are built from amino acids
The molecule is likely starch because starch monomers contain amino groups
explain: Amino-acid monomers and sulfur-containing side chains indicate a protein (e.g., cysteine, methionine).

text: A solution shifts from pH 8 to pH 6. Which statement is correct?
choices: The hydrogen ion concentration increased by 100 times
The hydrogen ion concentration decreased by 100 times
The solution became more basic
The pH change is too small to matter
explain: Two pH units correspond to a 10^2 (100x) change in [H+]; a drop from 8 to 6 increases [H+].

text: A mutation replaces a small amino acid in a tightly packed protein interior with a much larger amino acid. Which effect is most likely?
choices: The larger amino acid may disrupt packing and alter folding or stability
The mutation will definitely increase enzyme activity
The protein will become RNA
The protein will lose all covalent bonds
explain: Steric clashes from a bulkier residue in the core can destabilize folding and function.

text: A scientist compares two nucleic acid molecules. One is double-stranded and chemically stable for long-term storage. The other is usually single-stranded and more involved in immediate information transfer. Which conclusion is best supported?
choices: The first is DNA and the second is RNA
The first is a protein and the second is a carbohydrate
The first is RNA and the second is DNA
Both are lipids because they contain carbon
explain: DNA is typically double-stranded and stable for storage; RNA is often single-stranded and used in short-term information transfer.

text:
Students test an unknown solution with three indicators.

Benedict’s test: negative
Biuret test: positive
Iodine test: negative

Which conclusion is most reasonable?
choices: The sample most likely contains protein
The sample most likely contains starch
The sample most likely contains reducing sugar
The sample most likely contains only lipids
explain: A positive Biuret test indicates peptide bonds/proteins; the other indicators are negative.
experiment: Benedict: negative; Biuret: positive; Iodine: negative

text: A lab group compares two lipid samples. Sample A leaves a flexible membrane-like film in water, while Sample B collects into oily droplets. Chemical analysis shows Sample A has a phosphate-containing polar head group, while Sample B does not. Which conclusion is best supported?
choices: Sample A is likely composed of amphipathic phospholipids suited for membrane formation
Sample B must be a protein because it forms droplets
Sample A must be made of amino acids because it forms films
Sample B is more hydrophilic than Sample A
explain: A polar head with hydrophobic tails is characteristic of phospholipids that self-assemble into membranes.

text: Researchers compare two proteins with identical amino acid length. Protein 1 has multiple surface-exposed polar residues and functions in cytosol. Protein 2 has many surface-exposed nonpolar residues and is found spanning a membrane. Which conclusion is best supported?
choices: Protein structure is adapted to the environment in which the protein functions
Cytosolic proteins contain no nonpolar amino acids anywhere
Membrane proteins are made of lipids instead of amino acids
All proteins of the same length have the same function
explain: Surface chemistry that matches the environment helps proteins maintain stability and function.

text:
A scientist tests an enzyme at different temperatures.

10°C: low activity
25°C: moderate activity
37°C: highest activity
70°C: very low activity

Which interpretation is most reasonable?
choices: The enzyme has an optimal temperature near 37°C, and high temperature likely disrupts its structure
The enzyme becomes more active forever as temperature rises
The enzyme is a lipid because it changes with temperature
Temperature affects only the substrate, not the enzyme
explain: Enzymes have temperature optima; high temperatures can denature proteins and reduce activity.

text:
Students compare two solutions after adding the same amount of acid.

Solution M:
Initial pH 7.1
Final pH 6.9

Solution N:
Initial pH 7.1
Final pH 4.7

Which conclusion is best supported?
choices: Solution M has greater buffering capacity than Solution N
Solution N has greater buffering capacity because its pH changed more
Solution M contains no hydrogen ions
Solution N must contain more protein
explain: Smaller pH change indicates stronger buffering capacity; Solution M resists pH change more than N.

text: A mutation changes one codon so that a cysteine in a protein is replaced with a glycine. The protein becomes less stable under stressful conditions. Which explanation is most likely?
choices: The protein may have lost a disulfide bond that helped stabilize its structure
The protein must now be longer than before
The mutation changed the protein into DNA
Glycine always breaks peptide bonds
explain: Cysteine can form disulfide bonds that stabilize folding; replacing it with glycine removes that covalent stabilizer.

text: A researcher hydrolyzes a DNA molecule and identifies the resulting monomers. Which set of components should be present in those monomers?
choices: A sugar, a phosphate group, and a nitrogenous base
A glycerol, three fatty acids, and a phosphate group
An amino group, a carboxyl group, and an R group
Glucose monomers linked by glycosidic bonds
explain: Nucleotides consist of a pentose sugar, phosphate, and a nitrogenous base.

text: Students test two carbohydrate polymers made only of glucose. Polymer A is readily digested by a specific enzyme. Polymer B is not digested at all by that enzyme. Which explanation is best supported?
choices: The enzyme is specific for the bonding arrangement or shape present in Polymer A
Polymer B must contain no covalent bonds
Polymer A is really a protein
Enzymes digest all glucose polymers equally
explain: Enzyme active sites recognize precise linkages and three-dimensional arrangements; Polymer B lacks the recognized bond orientation.

text: A scientist compares two DNA samples of equal length. Sample A contains more G-C pairs and melts at a higher temperature than Sample B. Which explanation best fits the data?
choices: G-C rich DNA is often more thermally stable because G-C pairs form more hydrogen bonds
Sample A must contain ribose
Sample B contains no phosphodiester bonds
Only proteins melt when heated
explain: G-C base pairs form three hydrogen bonds (vs two for A-T), increasing thermal stability with higher G-C content.

text: A biologist studies an unknown biomolecule. It is made from monomers with amino and carboxyl groups, contains variable side chains, and its activity changes when its three-dimensional shape changes. Which conclusion is most supported?
choices: The molecule is a protein whose function depends on amino acid sequence and folding
The molecule is RNA because RNA contains amino and carboxyl groups
The molecule is a phospholipid because phospholipids have variable R groups
The molecule is starch because starch changes shape in water
explain: Amino-acid monomers with variable R groups that affect folding identify the molecule as a protein.

text: A protein normally binds a negatively charged substrate through a positively charged region in its active site. After mutation, one of those positive amino acids becomes negatively charged, and binding weakens. Which explanation is most reasonable?
choices: The mutation likely disrupted an electrostatic interaction important for substrate binding
The mutation must have added extra amino acids to the protein
The substrate must now be a lipid
The mutation proves active sites do not matter for enzyme function
explain: Reversing charge at the interface disrupts electrostatic attraction and reduces binding affinity.

text:
Researchers compare two water-based solutions after identical heating.

Solution A: +5°C
Solution B: +14°C

Which conclusion is best supported?
choices: Solution A likely has a higher specific heat than Solution B
Solution B absorbed no energy
Solution A must be basic
Solution B must contain only lipids
explain: Under equal energy input, a smaller temperature increase indicates a higher specific heat capacity.
